title: Day 46: Semana Santa Security Holds — No Major Cartel Incidents Reported; O3 Consolidation Advancing
detail: Day 46 since El Mencho's killing at Tapalpa, Jalisco (February 22, 2026). Operativo Semana Santa Segura 2026 enters its final four days (official conclusion: April 12), with 10,000+ security personnel deployed across CJNG-held Jalisco, Michoacán, Colima, and Nayarit. The holiday security corridor has held: no major cartel incidents were reported across Mexico's major tourist destinations during the Semana Santa peak — a significant security accomplishment for the Sheinbaum government demonstrating that the CJNG succession crisis has not translated into tourist-zone violence. O3 (Juan Carlos Valencia González) continues his succession consolidation under the collective council structure backed by Grupo Élite's paramilitary capacity. El Jardinero (Audias Flores Silva) — primary rival power center with a $5 million U.S. State Department bounty — remains at large with no confirmed change in status. The post-Semana Santa transition period (April 12 onwards) is being closely monitored by intelligence analysts as a potential resumption window for inter-cartel violence or succession-related posturing. Operativo Enjambre cumulative total remains at 67+ officials detained. U.S.-Mexico Joint Intelligence Targeting Center (JITC-CC) maintains high-tempo intelligence sharing.

title: CJNG Distribution Ring Sentenced in North Carolina — Cartel Reach Documented Into American Southeast
detail: Two Mexican nationals were sentenced in U.S. federal court on April 9, 2026 for trafficking kilogram quantities of methamphetamine across western North Carolina — with CJNG-branded clothing recovered from the ring leader, confirming direct cartel supply-chain affiliation. Lorenzo Lemus-Orozco, 42, the ring's organizer, was sentenced to 140 months (11.6 years) in federal prison followed by five years of supervised release; Jesús Miguel Ángel Gutiérrez-López, 53, a key distributor, received 70 months followed by one year of supervised release. Together they moved meth through Catawba, Caldwell, and Watauga counties in western North Carolina. CJNG-branded merchandise recovered from Lemus-Orozco's Hickory trailer provided investigators with direct cartel affiliation evidence. Both men will be deported upon completing their sentences. The case illustrates CJNG's penetration well beyond traditional Southwest border corridors into the American interior, part of the cartel's documented strategy of establishing distribution hubs in non-traditional markets with less rival network saturation. This sentencing comes 46 days after El Mencho's killing and two days after co-founder El 85's guilty plea — representing continued U.S. prosecution of CJNG's distribution architecture at multiple levels simultaneously.

sources.name: U.S. DOJ (Western District of North Carolina)
